narrative:
Dear Student,

I understand that receiving a rejection for your university application can be disheartening, and I want to help you understand the reasons behind this decision. The model used to evaluate your application predicted a 47% probability that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt. This prediction led to the rejection of your application, as the university aims to admit students who are likely to succeed in passing the bar exam on their first try.

Let's look at the factors that influenced this prediction. Your academic performance, particularly your grades in Year 1 and Year 3, were significant factors. Your Year 1 decile was lower than average, which means your grades were not as high as many of your peers. This was one of the strongest influences pushing the prediction toward a potential failure. Similarly, your LSAT score was below the average, which also contributed to the prediction that you might struggle with the bar exam.

On a positive note, your undergraduate GPA was slightly above average, which worked in your favor, suggesting that you have the potential to succeed academically. Additionally, your family income level and racial background were considered, but they had a less significant impact on the prediction compared to your academic performance and LSAT score.

The model also took into account your part-time status, which is less common among applicants, and your gender, which had a minimal influence on the prediction. The most influential factors were your Year 3 decile, LSAT score, and Year 1 decile, in that order, with each pushing the prediction toward a potential failure.

To improve your chances of passing the bar exam in the future, you might consider focusing on enhancing your LSAT score, as this is a critical component of the evaluation. Additionally, seeking opportunities to improve your academic performance in any future studies could also be beneficial. Remember, this prediction is not a definitive measure of your abilities, and with dedication and effort, you can certainly improve your prospects.

I hope this explanation helps you understand the decision and provides guidance on how to move forward. Please feel free to reach out if you have any further questions or need additional support.

Best regards,

[Your Name]
